name: Azrael
description: Azrael, also known as the Angel of Death, is a prominent figure in Abrahamic religions, particularly in Islam and some strands of Judaism. While not explicitly named in the Hebrew Bible, traditions surrounding an angel responsible for taking souls existed. In Islamic tradition, Azrael is one of the four archangels and is tasked with separating the soul from the body upon death. The process is often described as gentle for the righteous and agonizing for the wicked. Azrael is not portrayed as inherently malevolent, but rather as a divine agent carrying out God's will. Interpretations of Azrael vary across different Abrahamic texts and folk traditions. Some accounts depict Azrael as having eyes all over the body, allowing him to observe everyone simultaneously, while others emphasize his role as a compassionate guide leading souls to the afterlife. The angel's presence signifies the transition from earthly existence to the divine realm, a pivotal moment in the cycle of life and death within Abrahamic belief systems.
subtitle: Angel of Death

keyMyths.title: The Taking of Moses' Soul
keyMyths.description: One prominent myth recounts Azrael's attempt to take the soul of Moses. Moses, reluctant to die, struck Azrael, blinding him in one eye. God then restored Azrael's sight and instructed him to return to Moses with a handful of dust, promising that Moses would live as long as he held the dust. Upon seeing the dust, Moses understood the inevitability of death and willingly surrendered his soul to Azrael, demonstrating acceptance of divine will. This story highlights the themes of mortality and submission to God's plan.

keyMyths.title: Azrael's Tears
keyMyths.description: According to some Islamic traditions, Azrael weeps when taking the soul of a believer. These tears are not a sign of regret or sorrow for the act itself, but rather a reflection of the pain and suffering the soul experiences during the transition. The tears symbolize Azrael's compassion and empathy for humanity, even while fulfilling his divine duty. This myth emphasizes the delicate balance between divine command and emotional sensitivity in the portrayal of Azrael.

keyMyths.title: Azrael and the Flood
keyMyths.description: Some interpretations connect Azrael to the Great Flood. According to certain accounts, Azrael played a role in overseeing the flood's destruction, ensuring that all souls were properly taken. This narrative positions Azrael not just as an angel of individual death, but also as an agent of divine judgment on a grand scale. It reinforces the idea of Azrael as a powerful and essential component of God's cosmic order, capable of carrying out both personal and universal tasks.
keyMyths.source: Various Islamic interpretations of the Quran and Hadith
